report_metadata.cancer_type: Cervical Lymph Node Squamous Cell Carcinoma — Unknown Primary, HPV-Positive
report_metadata.stage: T0N1M0 (AJCC 8th Edition: Stage I for HPV-positive oropharyngeal primary)
molecular_profile: Presumed occult oropharyngeal primary (tonsil or tongue base)

treatments.name: Neck Dissection + Ipsilateral RT (60-66 Gy) with Concurrent Cisplatin

evidence_level.rationale: NCCN Category 2A recommendation for unknown primary HPV+ with N1 disease; supported by ASCO guideline (JCO 2020) and ASTRO clinical practice guideline (2024). Standard of care based on multiple retrospective series and guideline consensus.

survival_benefit.rationale: HPV-positive unknown primary T0N1 has excellent prognosis: 3-year OS ~94.8%, 5-year OS ~85-90%. Multimodality approach (surgery + adjuvant CRT) provides highest cure rates. Neck dissection provides pathologic staging to guide adjuvant therapy intensity.

accessibility.rationale: All components (neck dissection, IMRT, cisplatin) are FDA-approved, widely available at academic and community cancer centers. Cisplatin is generic and inexpensive. IMRT is standard radiation modality.

safety_profile.rationale: Cisplatin-based CRT causes ototoxicity (33%), nephrotoxicity, mucositis, dysphagia, nausea. Ipsilateral RT significantly reduces toxicity vs bilateral fields. Neck dissection risks include cranial nerve XI injury, shoulder dysfunction, lymphedema.

biomarker_match.rationale: HPV-positive status confirms favorable biology and expected excellent response to cisplatin-based chemoradiation. CPS>=20 not directly relevant for curative-intent CRT but confirms immunogenic tumor biology.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Neck dissection removes gross nodal disease and provides pathologic staging. Adjuvant radiation with concurrent cisplatin eliminates microscopic residual disease. Cisplatin is a DNA-crosslinking agent that potentiates radiation-induced DNA damage through inhibition of DNA repair. Ipsilateral RT targets the involved neck and putative oropharyngeal mucosal sites while sparing contralateral structures.

key_evidence.study_name: ASCO Guideline: Diagnosis and Management of SCC Unknown Primary Head and Neck

treatments.name: Neck Dissection + Ipsilateral RT Alone (60 Gy) Without Chemotherapy

evidence_level.rationale: NCCN-supported option for N1 disease without adverse pathologic features (no extranodal extension, clear margins). ASCO guideline recognizes reduced-intensity adjuvant approach for low-risk HPV+ unknown primary. Supported by multiple retrospective series.

survival_benefit.rationale: For T0N1 HPV+ without extranodal extension, adjuvant RT alone after neck dissection achieves 3-year OS >90% and 5-year disease-specific survival >85%. Omitting cisplatin acceptable for single node without adverse features per risk stratification.

accessibility.rationale: IMRT widely available at all radiation oncology centers. No systemic therapy costs. Neck dissection is a standard surgical procedure. Entire treatment available at community cancer centers.

safety_profile.rationale: Eliminating cisplatin removes ototoxicity, nephrotoxicity, and hematologic toxicity risks. Ipsilateral RT alone significantly reduces mucositis, xerostomia, and dysphagia compared to bilateral CRT. Better functional outcomes and quality of life.

biomarker_match.rationale: HPV-positive tumors are exquisitely radiosensitive, supporting effective tumor control with radiation alone in low-risk disease. Favorable biology allows chemotherapy omission without sacrificing cure rates.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Neck dissection provides definitive surgical treatment of gross nodal disease. Adjuvant ipsilateral RT at 60 Gy eliminates microscopic residual disease in the surgical bed and covers putative primary sites (ipsilateral tonsil fossa, tongue base). Radiation induces DNA double-strand breaks; HPV+ cells have impaired DNA repair, increasing radiosensitivity.

key_evidence.study_name: ASCO Guideline SCC Unknown Primary / Multiple retrospective series
key_evidence.journal: Journal of Clinical Oncology / Radiotherapy and Oncology

treatments.name: Definitive Ipsilateral IMRT (70 Gy) with Concurrent Cisplatin (Without Surgery)

evidence_level.rationale: NCCN Category 2A for definitive CRT in unknown primary. Supported by NRG-HN005 confirming 70 Gy + cisplatin remains standard; 2-year PFS 98.1%, OS 99%. De-escalation to 60 Gy did not meet noninferiority threshold. ASCO guideline supports ipsilateral fields for HPV+ unilateral disease.

survival_benefit.rationale: NRG-HN005 showed 2-year PFS 98.1% and OS 99% with standard-dose CRT. For HPV+ N1 disease, cure rates approach 95%. Ipsilateral radiation covers putative oropharyngeal primary sites while maintaining disease control equivalent to bilateral fields.

accessibility.rationale: IMRT and cisplatin available at all radiation oncology centers. No specialized surgical equipment required. Cisplatin is generic. This is a non-surgical option suitable for patients who decline or are not candidates for surgery.

safety_profile.rationale: 70 Gy + cisplatin is the most toxic standard option. Grade 3+ mucositis in 30-40%, ototoxicity 33%, nephrotoxicity, severe dysphagia requiring feeding tube in up to 42% with photon RT. However, ipsilateral fields significantly reduce contralateral salivary toxicity.

biomarker_match.rationale: HPV+ tumors show exquisite radiosensitivity. CPS>=20 not directly used in curative CRT decisions but indicates immunogenic phenotype. Standard cisplatin-based CRT is definitive therapy for HPV+ oropharyngeal-type disease.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Definitive intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) delivers 70 Gy in 33-35 fractions to gross nodal disease and 50-56 Gy to elective volumes covering putative mucosal primary sites (ipsilateral tonsil fossa, tongue base, soft palate). Concurrent high-dose cisplatin (100 mg/m2 q3w x 2-3 cycles or 40 mg/m2 weekly) radiosensitizes tumor cells through inhibition of DNA repair mechanisms.

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab Monotherapy (for Recurrent/Metastatic Setting, CPS >= 20)

evidence_level.rationale: KEYNOTE-048 Phase III RCT demonstrated significant OS benefit for pembrolizumab monotherapy in CPS>=20 R/M HNSCC. FDA-approved first-line for CPS>=1. Updated 5-year follow-up data published 2025 confirming durable benefit. Level I evidence.

survival_benefit.rationale: For CPS>=20 R/M HNSCC: median OS 14.9 months (pembrolizumab) vs 10.7 months (cetuximab-chemo), HR 0.61. Durable responses in responders. However, this is relevant only for recurrent/metastatic disease, not primary curative-intent treatment. T0N1 patients have >90% cure rate with primary treatment.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved first-line for R/M HNSCC with CPS>=1. Widely available at oncology centers. Standard dosing 200 mg IV q3w or 400 mg q6w. Insurance coverage established.

safety_profile.rationale: Grade 3-4 treatment-related AEs only 17% with pembrolizumab monotherapy vs 69% with cetuximab-chemo. Immune-related AEs manageable: thyroiditis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, colitis. Better quality of life than chemotherapy. Generally well-tolerated.

biomarker_match.rationale: CPS>=20 is the optimal biomarker for pembrolizumab monotherapy response. Patient's CPS>=20 status matches the subgroup with greatest benefit (HR 0.61). HPV+ tumors tend to be immunogenic with higher PD-L1 expression, further supporting checkpoint inhibitor efficacy.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pembrolizumab is a humanized IgG4 monoclonal antibody targeting PD-1 (programmed death-1) receptor on T lymphocytes. By blocking PD-1/PD-L1 interaction, it restores anti-tumor T-cell immune responses. HPV-positive tumors express viral antigens (E6, E7 oncoproteins) that serve as neo-antigens, making them particularly susceptible to immune checkpoint blockade. CPS>=20 indicates high PD-L1 expression in the tumor microenvironment.

key_evidence.journal: European Journal of Cancer / Journal of Clinical Oncology

treatments.availability: FDA Approved — First-line for R/M HNSCC (CPS >= 1)

treatments.name: Neck Dissection + De-Escalated Adjuvant RT (50 Gy) Without Chemotherapy (ECOG-3311 Approach)

evidence_level.rationale: ECOG-ACRIN E3311 Phase II trial showed 50 Gy adjuvant RT without chemotherapy achieved 94.9% 3-year disease-free survival in intermediate-risk patients. Not yet validated in Phase III. Specifically designed for surgical-first approach with risk-directed adjuvant therapy.

survival_benefit.rationale: E3311 intermediate-risk arm: 94.9% 3-year DFS, 54-month PFS 90.6%, OS 95.3%. For T0N1 without adverse features, outcomes comparable to full-dose CRT. Preserves functional outcomes while maintaining high cure rates.

accessibility.rationale: Requires TORS-capable center for surgical component. 50 Gy RT is lower dose, reducing treatment burden. No chemotherapy costs. Currently transitioning from trial to accepted practice at experienced centers.

safety_profile.rationale: Reduced radiation dose (50 vs 60-70 Gy) significantly decreases mucositis, dysphagia, and xerostomia. No chemotherapy eliminates ototoxicity, nephrotoxicity, and hematologic toxicity. E3311 showed reduced aspiration rates with 50 Gy vs 60 Gy at 6 months.

biomarker_match.rationale: Designed specifically for HPV+ oropharyngeal cancer. HPV-positive biology with radiosensitivity supports efficacy at lower radiation doses. Requires favorable surgical pathology (negative margins, <5 positive nodes, no major ENE).

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Transoral robotic surgery with neck dissection provides definitive surgical treatment. Risk stratification based on pathologic features directs adjuvant therapy intensity. Intermediate-risk patients (close margins, 2-4 positive nodes, minor ENE) receive 50 Gy IMRT without chemotherapy, exploiting HPV+ tumor radiosensitivity at lower doses. Low-risk patients (clear margins, single small node) may undergo observation alone.
treatments.availability: Phase II evidence — Adopted at experienced centers

treatments.name: TORS Tonsillectomy + Tongue Base Mucosectomy + Neck Dissection (Surgical-Only Approach)

evidence_level.rationale: Supported by ECOG-ACRIN E3311 trial framework and multiple institutional series. ASCO guideline recommends bilateral tonsillectomy and tongue base mucosectomy as diagnostic procedure. If primary identified and resected with clear margins + favorable pathology, surgery alone may suffice for select T0N1 patients.

survival_benefit.rationale: Long-term outcomes show 5-year OS ~91% for HPV+ oropharyngeal cancers treated with TORS + neck dissection. Primary identification rate 72-90% with combined tonsillectomy and mucosectomy. Allows risk-directed adjuvant therapy, with some patients needing no further treatment.

accessibility.rationale: TORS requires robotic surgical platform (da Vinci system) and experienced surgeon. Available at major academic centers but limited at community hospitals. Transoral laser microsurgery (TLM) is an alternative where TORS unavailable.

safety_profile.rationale: TORS for unknown primary has low complication rates. Avoids radiation in some patients entirely, eliminating long-term xerostomia, dysphagia, and osteoradionecrosis risks. Surgical risks include hemorrhage (2-4%), temporary dysphagia, velopharyngeal insufficiency.

biomarker_match.rationale: HPV+ status strongly predicts oropharyngeal primary (95.7% sensitivity, 85.7% specificity), directing tonsillectomy and tongue base mucosectomy as targeted diagnostic-therapeutic procedure.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Transoral robotic surgery (TORS) enables en-bloc resection of bilateral palatine tonsils and lingual tonsil (tongue base mucosectomy) to identify and resect the occult primary tumor. Combined with selective neck dissection (levels II-IV), this approach is both diagnostic and potentially curative. If the primary is identified with clear margins and pathology shows no adverse features, adjuvant therapy can be de-escalated or omitted.

key_evidence.study_name: ECOG-ACRIN E3311 / Systematic review on TORS for CUP
key_evidence.journal: Journal of Clinical Oncology / Head & Neck

treatments.availability: FDA Approved — Available at academic/robotic surgery centers

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab + Cisplatin/5-FU Combination (for Recurrent/Metastatic Setting)

evidence_level.rationale: KEYNOTE-048 Phase III RCT: pembrolizumab + cisplatin/carboplatin + 5-FU demonstrated significant OS benefit vs cetuximab-chemo across all PD-L1 subgroups. FDA-approved first-line for R/M HNSCC. 5-year follow-up data published 2025.

survival_benefit.rationale: CPS>=20: median OS 14.7 months (pembro+chemo) vs 11.0 months (cetuximab-chemo), HR 0.60. For CPS>=1: HR 0.65. Higher ORR than pembrolizumab monotherapy. Reserved for R/M disease; T0N1 patients rarely reach this setting with adequate primary treatment.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved first-line. All components widely available. Cisplatin and 5-FU are generic. Pembrolizumab available through established oncology channels. Requires infusion center visits.

safety_profile.rationale: Combined immunotherapy + chemotherapy increases toxicity burden. Grade 3-4 AEs in ~70% (similar to cetuximab-chemo). Chemotherapy-related: cytopenias, mucositis, diarrhea, nausea. Plus immune-related AEs. Higher response rate may justify toxicity in select patients.

biomarker_match.rationale: CPS>=20 shows excellent benefit. HPV+ tumors highly immunogenic. Combination approach benefits broader population (CPS>=1) compared to monotherapy. Patient's CPS>=20 and HPV+ status optimize response potential.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1) restores anti-tumor immunity. Cisplatin induces DNA crosslinks causing tumor cell death and immunogenic cell death that enhances immune recognition. 5-Fluorouracil inhibits thymidylate synthase, blocking DNA/RNA synthesis. The combination leverages immunotherapy-chemotherapy synergy: chemotherapy-induced tumor death releases neoantigens that enhance pembrolizumab-mediated T-cell responses.

key_evidence.study_name: KEYNOTE-048 Phase III Trial (Combination Arm, 5-year update)

treatments.availability: FDA Approved — First-line for R/M HNSCC

treatments.name: Nivolumab (Second-Line for Recurrent/Metastatic Disease)

evidence_level.rationale: CheckMate 141 Phase III RCT demonstrated significant OS benefit for nivolumab vs investigator's choice in platinum-refractory R/M HNSCC. FDA-approved 2016. Long-term 2-year follow-up confirms durable benefit. HR for death 0.68.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median OS 7.5 months (nivolumab) vs 5.1 months (control), HR 0.70. 24-month OS rate 16.9% vs 6.0%. Modest absolute benefit but meaningful in platinum-refractory setting. Second-line agent; most T0N1 HPV+ patients are cured and never reach this scenario.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for R/M HNSCC after platinum-based chemotherapy. Widely available at oncology centers. Standard dosing 240 mg IV q2w or 480 mg q4w. Established insurance coverage.

safety_profile.rationale: Grade 3-4 AEs only 13.1% (nivolumab) vs 35.1% (standard therapy). Significantly lower toxicity than chemotherapy alternatives. Quality of life maintained. Immune-related AEs manageable.

biomarker_match.rationale: OS benefit seen across HPV+ and HPV- subgroups. PD-L1>=1% showed greater benefit (HR 0.55) but benefit also seen in PD-L1<1% (HR 0.73). Not as biomarker-selective as pembrolizumab in CPS>=20 setting. Patient's HPV+ and CPS>=20 predict favorable response.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Nivolumab is a fully human IgG4 monoclonal antibody targeting PD-1 receptor. It blocks PD-1/PD-L1 and PD-1/PD-L2 interactions, restoring anti-tumor T-cell activity. In HPV+ cancers, viral oncoproteins E6 and E7 serve as immunogenic neo-antigens, providing targets for reinvigorated T-cell responses.

key_evidence.journal: New England Journal of Medicine / Oral Oncology

biomarker_requirements: No specific PD-L1 threshold required for FDA approval
treatments.availability: FDA Approved — Second-line for R/M HNSCC

treatments.name: Neck Dissection Alone (Observation After Surgery — Select Low-Risk T0N1)

evidence_level.rationale: NCCN acknowledges observation after neck dissection for single small node without ECE, but with limited prospective data. Supported by select retrospective series showing low recurrence in truly low-risk HPV+ T0N1 patients. ECOG E3311 low-risk arm included observation. Not validated in Phase III RCT.

survival_benefit.rationale: For carefully selected patients (single node <3cm, no ECE, clear margins on tonsillectomy/mucosectomy, primary identified and resected), recurrence rates are very low (<5-10%). However, risk of occult residual disease means close surveillance is essential. Some patients may require salvage treatment.

accessibility.rationale: No adjuvant therapy required — lowest treatment burden option. After surgery, only requires close surveillance with clinical exams and imaging. Eliminates radiation and chemotherapy costs and logistics entirely.

safety_profile.rationale: Avoids all radiation and chemotherapy toxicity. No mucositis, xerostomia, dysphagia, ototoxicity, or nephrotoxicity from adjuvant treatment. Only surgical risks (shoulder dysfunction, lymphedema). Best quality-of-life preservation of all options.

biomarker_match.rationale: HPV+ favorable biology supports observation in lowest-risk subgroup. Requires primary identified and resected during tonsillectomy/mucosectomy with clear margins. Strict pathologic criteria limit eligibility.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Selective neck dissection (levels II-IV) removes all involved lymph node(s) with surrounding fibrofatty tissue. If bilateral tonsillectomy and tongue base mucosectomy identifies and completely resects the occult primary with clear margins, and pathology shows favorable features (single small node, no extranodal extension), the surgical resection alone may be curative. Close surveillance detects any recurrence early for timely salvage therapy.

key_evidence.study_name: ECOG E3311 Low-Risk Arm / Institutional retrospective series
key_evidence.journal: Journal of Clinical Oncology / Otolaryngology-Head and Neck Surgery

biomarker_requirements: Primary identified and resected with clear margins
Patient compliant with close surveillance protocol
notable_side_effects: Shoulder dysfunction (cranial nerve XI injury risk)
treatments.availability: Standard surgical procedure — Observation approach at select academic centers

treatments.name: Cetuximab + RT (for Cisplatin-Ineligible Patients)

evidence_level.rationale: RTOG 1016 and De-ESCALaTE HPV Phase III trials demonstrated cetuximab-RT is inferior to cisplatin-RT for HPV+ OPC. However, cetuximab-RT remains NCCN-recommended alternative for patients unable to tolerate cisplatin (renal insufficiency, hearing loss, neuropathy, poor PS).

survival_benefit.rationale: RTOG 1016: 10-year OS 66.5% (cetuximab) vs 68.7% (cisplatin), HR 1.18 (did not meet noninferiority). Still provides >65% 10-year OS for HPV+ patients. Appropriate alternative when cisplatin toxicity is unacceptable. For T0N1, cure rates remain high even with cetuximab-RT.

accessibility.rationale: Cetuximab is FDA-approved and widely available. Weekly IV infusion during radiation. Available at community and academic centers. Moderate cost (biologic agent).

safety_profile.rationale: Lower ototoxicity (17% vs 33%), no nephrotoxicity, no significant neuropathy compared to cisplatin. However, cetuximab causes acneiform rash (80%+), radiation dermatitis enhancement, infusion reactions (3-5% severe). Overall better tolerated than cisplatin for most patients.

biomarker_match.rationale: EGFR universally expressed in HNSCC. HPV+ status does not predict differential cetuximab benefit. RTOG 1016 showed cetuximab inferior specifically in HPV+ population. Reserved for cisplatin-ineligible patients regardless of HPV or CPS status.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cetuximab is a chimeric IgG1 monoclonal antibody targeting the epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR). It blocks EGF binding, inhibiting downstream RAS/MAPK and PI3K/AKT signaling cascades that drive cell proliferation and survival. Cetuximab also mediates antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC). When combined with radiation, EGFR inhibition impairs DNA repair and enhances radiation-induced apoptosis.

key_evidence.study_name: NRG Oncology RTOG 1016 Phase III Trial (10-year update)
key_evidence.journal: The Lancet / Journal of Clinical Oncology

treatments.availability: FDA Approved — Alternative to cisplatin for cisplatin-ineligible patients

treatments.name: Proton Beam Therapy (Intensity-Modulated Proton Therapy) with Concurrent Cisplatin

evidence_level.rationale: Prospective observational data from MD Anderson (n=14) showed excellent outcomes for unknown primary. Phase III TORPEdO and IMPT vs IMRT trials ongoing. Randomized phase III at ASCO 2024 showed reduction in feeding tube rates (28% vs 42%). Not yet level I evidence for superiority.

survival_benefit.rationale: 2-year locoregional control 100%, OS 90%, DFS 79% for proton-treated HNCUP. Disease control comparable to photon IMRT. Primary benefit is toxicity reduction rather than survival improvement, which preserves quality of life in long-surviving HPV+ patients.

accessibility.rationale: Proton therapy centers are limited (approximately 40 in the US). Requires specialized, expensive infrastructure. Insurance coverage variable. Geographic access barrier for many patients. Wait times can be significant.

safety_profile.rationale: Significantly reduced toxicity vs photon IMRT: grade 3 oral mucositis only 7% (vs 52% IMRT), 0% feeding tube dependence, xerostomia 6% vs 20% at 18-24 months. 93% met QUANTEC parotid-sparing criteria vs 32% with IMRT. Superior quality of life preservation.

biomarker_match.rationale: HPV+ tumors may show enhanced sensitivity to proton therapy. Young HPV+ patients with long life expectancy benefit most from reduced late toxicity. Particularly relevant for unknown primary where mucosal coverage fields increase normal tissue exposure.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Intensity-modulated proton therapy (IMPT) delivers radiation using proton particles that deposit maximum energy at a specific depth (Bragg peak), then stop. This eliminates exit dose beyond the target, dramatically reducing radiation to normal tissues including contralateral parotid glands, oral cavity, larynx, and pharyngeal constrictors. Combined with concurrent cisplatin for radiosensitization.

key_evidence.study_name: Proton Beam Therapy for Head and Neck CUP (MD Anderson Prospective Series)
key_evidence.journal: International Journal of Particle Therapy / ASCO 2024

biomarker_requirements: p16-positive (HPV+) — primary indication for toxicity reduction
No specific biomarker requirement for eligibility
notable_side_effects: Mucositis (grade 3 in 7% — substantially lower than IMRT)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved — Limited to proton therapy centers (~40 US centers)

treatments.name: Circulating Tumor HPV-DNA (cfHPV-DNA) Guided Adaptive Therapy

evidence_level.rationale: Prospective studies demonstrate cfHPV-DNA strongly correlates with treatment response in HPV+ OPC. Patients with >50% cfHPV-DNA reduction at mid-treatment achieved 100% 1-year PFS with de-escalated therapy. Concept validated but adaptive protocols still in clinical trials.

survival_benefit.rationale: cfHPV-DNA-guided de-escalation: patients with >50% reduction received 50 Gy CRT with 100% 1-year PFS. Those with <30% reduction required standard 70 Gy, 1-year PFS 75%. Potential to personalize treatment intensity based on real-time biomarker response. Maintains cure rates while reducing toxicity in good responders.

accessibility.rationale: cfHPV-DNA assays available at specialized laboratories but not yet standard clinical practice. Adaptive treatment protocols require multidisciplinary coordination. Currently limited to clinical trial settings. Commercial assays in development.

safety_profile.rationale: The approach itself enables toxicity reduction by identifying patients who can safely receive less treatment. Blood-based biomarker monitoring is minimally invasive. De-escalated arm achieves lower mucositis, dysphagia, and xerostomia rates.

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly leverages HPV-positive status as a measurable circulating biomarker. Patient's HPV+ tumor sheds quantifiable HPV-DNA into plasma, enabling real-time treatment response monitoring. Uniquely applicable to HPV+ cancers.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cell-free HPV-DNA (cfHPV-DNA) is released into the bloodstream by HPV-positive tumor cells through apoptosis, necrosis, and active secretion. Serial quantification of cfHPV-DNA during treatment provides a real-time liquid biopsy readout of tumor response. Patients demonstrating rapid and profound cfHPV-DNA clearance (>50% reduction by mid-treatment) can be safely de-escalated to lower radiation doses, while non-responders receive standard-intensity therapy. This adaptive approach personalizes treatment intensity.
biomarker_requirements: Baseline cfHPV-DNA level above assay threshold
Access to validated cfHPV-DNA quantification assay
notable_side_effects: No side effects from the monitoring approach itself
Treatment toxicity depends on whether patient is de-escalated or receives standard therapy
Risk of under-treatment if biomarker interpretation is incorrect

treatments.name: ISA101b (Peltopepimut-S) HPV16 Therapeutic Vaccine + Checkpoint Inhibitor

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II OpcemISA trial (n=198) showed ISA101b + cemiplimab improved ORR and OS vs cemiplimab alone in CPS>=20 subgroup. Phase II ISA101 + nivolumab showed 33% ORR. However, no phase III validation. Results mixed in overall population.

survival_benefit.rationale: ISA101 + nivolumab: median OS 15.3 months in R/M HPV16+ OPC. OpcemISA: CPS>=20 subgroup showed improved ORR (25.3% vs 22.9% overall, but significant in CPS>=20). In anti-PD-1 refractory patients: ORR 11.5%, median OS 8.1 months. Moderate benefit in R/M setting.

accessibility.rationale: Investigational agent available only through clinical trials. Not FDA-approved. Limited trial sites. ISA101b manufactured by ISA Pharmaceuticals. Phase III trial planning underway for CPS>=20 subgroup.

safety_profile.rationale: Therapeutic vaccine is generally well-tolerated. Injection site reactions are the primary vaccine-related AE. Checkpoint inhibitor component carries standard immune-related AE profile. No dose-limiting vaccine toxicities reported.

biomarker_match.rationale: Specifically designed for HPV16-positive tumors. Patient's HPV+ and CPS>=20 status aligns with the subgroup showing greatest benefit in OpcemISA trial. Targets HPV16 E6 and E7 oncoproteins directly.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: ISA101b (peltopepimut-S) is a synthetic long peptide (SLP) vaccine consisting of overlapping peptides covering the full-length HPV16 E6 and E7 oncoproteins. The SLP vaccine is taken up by dendritic cells, processed, and presented on MHC class I and II molecules, inducing both CD4+ and CD8+ T-cell responses against HPV16-expressing tumor cells. Combination with checkpoint inhibitors (cemiplimab or nivolumab) removes PD-1-mediated T-cell exhaustion, amplifying vaccine-induced immune responses.

key_evidence.study_name: OpcemISA Phase II RCT / ISA101+Nivolumab Phase II

notable_side_effects: No dose-limiting toxicities from vaccine component

treatments.name: PDS0101 Therapeutic HPV Vaccine + Pembrolizumab (Neoadjuvant Window-of-Opportunity)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II window-of-opportunity trial (MC200710) presented at ASCO 2025. Early-stage data showing immune activation and pathologic response in treatment-naive HPV+ OPC. Novel neoadjuvant immunotherapy concept. No survival data yet.

survival_benefit.rationale: Window-of-opportunity design measures immune and pathologic responses before definitive treatment. Potential to identify patients who may benefit from treatment de-escalation based on immune response. No OS/PFS data available yet — early proof-of-concept.

accessibility.rationale: Investigational agent available only at select trial sites. PDS0101 manufactured by PDS Biotechnology. Not FDA-approved. Limited enrollment capacity.

safety_profile.rationale: Neoadjuvant window-of-opportunity design limits treatment exposure. PDS0101 uses Versamune liposomal delivery platform, generally well-tolerated. Pembrolizumab safety profile well-established. Given before standard surgery, minimal additional risk.

biomarker_match.rationale: Specifically targets HPV16 E6/E7 with Versamune-enhanced T-cell activation. Patient's HPV+ and CPS>=20 status ideal for vaccine-immunotherapy combination. Designed for treatment-naive locally advanced HPV+ OPC.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: PDS0101 is a liposomal HPV therapeutic vaccine using the Versamune platform (cationic lipid nanoparticles) to deliver HPV16 E6/E7 peptides. The liposomal delivery enhances dendritic cell uptake and cross-presentation, generating potent HPV-specific CD8+ T-cell responses. Combined with pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1) in a neoadjuvant window before surgery or definitive treatment to assess immunologic and pathologic tumor responses.
notable_side_effects: Minimal additional toxicity beyond standard care

treatments.name: PRGN-2009 HPV Vaccine + Cisplatin/Docetaxel Induction (Neoadjuvant)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase I/II trial (NCT06223568) currently recruiting at NCI. Gorilla adenovector-based HPV vaccine platform. No efficacy results published yet. Novel viral vector vaccine approach for HPV+ OPC. Preclinical data supports concept.

survival_benefit.rationale: No clinical survival data yet available. Study evaluating immune responses and pathologic complete response rates in newly diagnosed HPV+ OPC. Potential to enable treatment de-escalation based on immunologic response to induction.

accessibility.rationale: Phase I/II trial at NCI (National Cancer Institute). Very limited enrollment. Single-center or few-center trial. Not available outside research setting.

safety_profile.rationale: Gorilla adenovector vaccine technology has acceptable safety profile from other applications. Combined with standard induction chemotherapy (cisplatin/docetaxel) which carries significant toxicity: cytopenias, mucositis, nausea, neuropathy, alopecia.

biomarker_match.rationale: Specifically designed for HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer. PRGN-2009 targets HPV16/18 E6 and E7 oncoproteins. Patient's HPV+ status is required eligibility criterion.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: PRGN-2009 is a gorilla adenovirus-vectored therapeutic vaccine encoding modified HPV16 and HPV18 E6/E7 fusion proteins. The adenoviral vector infects antigen-presenting cells, expressing HPV antigens intracellularly for efficient MHC class I presentation and robust CD8+ cytotoxic T-cell responses. Combined with cisplatin/docetaxel induction chemotherapy to provide immunogenic cell death that synergizes with vaccine-induced anti-HPV immunity.
notable_side_effects: Vaccine-related: injection site reactions, fever, myalgia

clinical_trials.title: NRG-HN005: De-Intensified Radiation Therapy with Cisplatin or Nivolumab for Early-Stage HPV-Positive Oropharyngeal Cancer
biomarker_requirements: T1-T2 N1-N2b M0 (7th ed) or Stage I-II (8th ed)
clinical_trials.early_results: Standard arm (70 Gy + cisplatin) achieved 2-year PFS 98.1%, OS 99%. De-escalation with 60 Gy + nivolumab yielded 2-year PFS 90.3%, OS 96.1%. Neither de-escalation arm met noninferiority threshold. Trial halted — standard CRT remains superior.

clinical_trials.title: PRGN-2009 HPV Vaccine with Cisplatin/Docetaxel Induction in Newly Diagnosed HPV-Positive Oropharyngeal Cancer
clinical_trials.early_results: Gorilla adenovirus-vectored HPV vaccine targeting E6/E7. No efficacy data yet. Evaluating immunologic responses and pathologic complete response rates.

clinical_trials.title: HPV Therapeutic Vaccine with Pembrolizumab for p16+ Tonsil or Tongue Base Cancer
clinical_trials.early_results: Combining immunotherapeutic anti-HPV vaccine with pembrolizumab. Evaluating immune response and tumor regression. No published results yet.

clinical_trials.title: OpcemISA: Cemiplimab with or without ISA101b in Recurrent/Metastatic HPV16-Positive Oropharyngeal Cancer
clinical_trials.early_results: 198 patients enrolled. Overall ORR 25.3% (ISA101b+cemiplimab) vs 22.9% (cemiplimab). CPS>=20 subgroup showed significant improvement in ORR and OS with triple-dose ISA101b. CPS<20 patients did not benefit.

clinical_trials.title: RoboCUP: Bilateral Tongue Base Mucosectomy by TORS or TLM with Tonsillectomy for Head and Neck Cancer of Unknown Primary
clinical_trials.early_results: Randomized trial evaluating whether adding tongue base mucosectomy to tonsillectomy improves primary tumor identification rate. No results yet. Aims to establish standardized diagnostic surgical protocol.

clinical_trials.title: TORPEdO: Toxicity Reduction using Proton beam therapy for Oropharyngeal cancer
clinical_trials.early_results: Randomized comparison of IMPT vs IMRT with concurrent systemic therapy. Primary endpoint is late toxicity (xerostomia, dysphagia). Accrual ongoing. No interim results published.

combination_strategies.rationale: Surgery provides pathologic staging to tailor adjuvant intensity. Low-risk (clear margins, single small node, no ENE): observation. Intermediate-risk: 50 Gy RT alone. High-risk (positive margins, ENE): 60-66 Gy + cisplatin. This risk-adapted strategy maximizes cure while minimizing overtreatment in HPV+ patients with favorable biology.

Cisplatin-induced immunogenic cell death releases neoantigens. Concurrent PD-1 blockade with pembrolizumab may enhance anti-tumor T-cell response during chemoradiation. Phase IB data shows safety and feasibility. JAVELIN Head and Neck 100 (avelumab) failed in similar design; careful patient selection needed.

HPV vaccines generate tumor-specific T-cells targeting E6/E7 oncoproteins. PD-1 blockade prevents T-cell exhaustion, amplifying vaccine-induced immune responses. OpcemISA showed synergy in CPS>=20 subgroup. ISA101+nivolumab achieved 33% ORR in R/M HPV16+ OPC. Dual immune activation via antigen presentation and checkpoint release.

IMPT delivers equivalent tumor dose while dramatically reducing normal tissue exposure. Combined with radiosensitizing cisplatin, maintains high cure rates (2-year LRC 100%) while reducing toxicity: grade 3 mucositis 7% (vs 52% IMRT), 0% feeding tube dependence. Particularly beneficial for unknown primary where bilateral mucosal coverage increases normal tissue dose with photon RT.

Real-time cfHPV-DNA kinetics during treatment identify early responders who can safely receive reduced radiation doses (50 Gy vs 70 Gy). Mid-treatment cfHPV-DNA reduction >50% predicted 100% 1-year PFS with de-escalated therapy. Personalizes treatment intensity using tumor-specific biomarker rather than one-size-fits-all approach.

supportive_care.approach: Prophylactic Swallowing Exercises and Speech-Language Pathology
supportive_care.evidence: Multiple prospective studies and meta-analyses demonstrate that prophylactic swallowing exercises initiated before and during radiation therapy reduce severity and duration of post-treatment dysphagia. IOPI (Iowa Oral Performance Instrument) and EMST (Expiratory Muscle Strength Training) target tongue and expiratory muscle strength. MSK guidelines recommend exercises to strengthen neck-turning, mouth-opening, and swallowing throughout treatment.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduced feeding tube dependence (30-50% relative reduction), faster swallowing recovery, improved long-term swallowing function scores (MDADI), reduced aspiration pneumonia risk. Particularly important for HPV+ patients with long life expectancy who will live with treatment sequelae for decades.

supportive_care.approach: Intensive Nutritional Counseling and Oral Nutrition Supplements
supportive_care.evidence: ESPEN and ASCO guidelines recommend intensive dietary counseling for all head and neck cancer patients undergoing radiation. Studies show patients losing >=10% body weight have significantly worse outcomes including higher acute dysphagia, mucositis, and toxicity grades. Proactive nutritional assessment and intervention improve treatment tolerance and outcomes.
supportive_care.benefit: Prevention of >=10% weight loss during treatment, maintenance of adequate caloric/protein intake, reduced treatment breaks, improved treatment tolerance, faster post-treatment recovery. HPV+ patients may have higher acute toxicity from mucositis requiring proactive nutritional support.

supportive_care.approach: Oral Mucositis Prevention and Management Protocol
supportive_care.evidence: MASCC/ISOO guidelines recommend cryotherapy during bolus chemotherapy, low-level laser therapy (photobiomodulation), and basic oral care protocols. Evidence supports honey, oral glutamine, and probiotics for reducing radiation-induced mucositis severity. Benzydamine mouthwash reduces mucositis in moderate-dose radiation.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduction in grade 3-4 mucositis incidence by 30-50% with photobiomodulation. Reduced pain scores, decreased opioid requirements, fewer treatment interruptions. Maintained oral intake reduces feeding tube placement rates.

supportive_care.approach: Structured Exercise Program During and After Treatment
supportive_care.evidence: Meta-analyses (Cochrane review and others) demonstrate that structured exercise programs during cancer treatment improve fatigue, physical function, quality of life, and potentially survival. ACSM guidelines recommend 150 min/week moderate-intensity aerobic activity plus resistance training for cancer patients. Head and neck specific: neck and shoulder rehabilitation exercises post-neck dissection.
supportive_care.benefit: 30-40% reduction in cancer-related fatigue, improved cardiorespiratory fitness, maintained muscle mass during treatment, reduced depression and anxiety, improved treatment completion rates. Post-neck dissection shoulder rehabilitation preserves range of motion and reduces disability.

supportive_care.evidence: NCCN Distress Management guidelines recommend routine psychosocial screening for all cancer patients. Tobacco cessation is critical: smoking during radiation reduces cure rates by 50% and increases toxicity. HPV+ patients who smoke have worse outcomes (RTOG prognostic classification). Cognitive behavioral therapy and pharmacotherapy (varenicline/bupropion) are evidence-based cessation interventions.
supportive_care.benefit: Smoking cessation improves treatment response, reduces second primary cancer risk, improves overall survival. Psychosocial support reduces distress, improves treatment adherence, and enhances quality of life. Particularly important for 45yo male who may face body image, swallowing, and communication concerns.

supportive_care.approach: Dental Evaluation and Fluoride Therapy Pre-Radiation
supportive_care.evidence: NCCN and ADA guidelines mandate comprehensive dental evaluation before head and neck radiation. Teeth in radiation field require assessment for extraction vs preservation. Custom fluoride trays with daily high-concentration fluoride application prevent radiation caries. Hyperbaric oxygen may be needed for post-radiation dental extractions.
supportive_care.benefit: Prevention of radiation caries (can occur within months without fluoride), avoidance of osteoradionecrosis from post-radiation extractions, maintenance of dental function. Ipsilateral RT reduces but does not eliminate dental radiation exposure.
